description:
You are working as a Site-Reliability Engineer (SRE).  
    Your manager has asked you for a *one-off* point-in-time health-check of a public endpoint.  
    1. Probe the URL https://example.com/ (exactly that URL, including the trailing slash).  
    2. Capture the following three data points from a single curl execution:  
       • the current UTC timestamp in extended ISO-8601 form (YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ)  
       • the HTTP status code returned by the request  
       • the total time for the request in milliseconds, rounded to the nearest integer  
    3. Log the result to a new file located at  
       /home/user/uptime/health_check.log  
       (create the directory if it does not yet exist).  
    4. The log file must contain exactly **one** line whose *entire* contents are a JSON object with keys in THIS precise order and spelling:  
       {"timestamp":"<ISO8601>","status_code":<integer>,"response_time_ms":<integer>}  
       • No surrounding white-space.  
       • Keys must appear exactly as shown, separated by commas.  
       • The status_code must be an integer, not a string.  
       • The file must end with a single newline character.  

    Example of a syntactically valid line (values will differ):  
       {"timestamp":"2024-01-31T14:58:22Z","status_code":200,"response_time_ms":123}

    Nothing else should be written to the file or the console except whatever curl normally prints.  

    Deliverables that the automated grader will inspect:  
    • The file /home/user/uptime/health_check.log exists.  
    • The file contains exactly one line, in the format described above.  
    • The status_code value is 200.
truth:
Expected artefacts after successful completion:

    1. Directory:
       /home/user/uptime
       (regular directory, owned by user, default permissions 0755).

    2. File:
       /home/user/uptime/health_check.log
       Content: one line, ending in a single newline.

       The grader will validate the line with this PCRE-style expression  
       ^\{"timestamp":"[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z","status_code":200,"response_time_ms":[0-9]+\}\n$

       • Any timestamp that matches the date-time pattern is accepted.  
       • The status_code must be 200.  
       • response_time_ms must be one or more digits.  

    Note: example.com reliably returns HTTP 200 in our execution environment, so the agent’s curl call must record 200 as the status_code field.
name: task_000000_015c9641
